title: Peptides: The Molecular Alphabet
subtitle: How 20 amino acids combine to build the proteins that power life

children.children: This tutorial explores how amino acids link together to form peptides and proteins. You'll learn the chemical structure, how peptide bonds form, and the four levels of protein organization that turn simple chains into molecular machines.
What Are Amino Acids?

Amino acids are organic molecules that serve as the fundamental building blocks of proteins. There are 20 standard amino acids encoded by DNA, and despite this small alphabet, they combine to create over 100,000 different proteins in the human body.

Think of amino acids like letters in an alphabet. Just as 26 letters can form hundreds of thousands of English words, 20 amino acids can form virtually unlimited protein 'words.' The sequence matters enormously — just as 'STOP' and 'POTS' use the same letters but mean different things, the same amino acids in different orders create completely different proteins.
The Four Types of Amino Acids
Amino acids are classified by their side chain (R group) properties, which determine how they interact with water and other molecules:

rows: Glycine, Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Proline
Serine, Threonine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Asparagine, Glutamine

children.children: Nonpolar amino acids have hydrocarbon side chains that don't interact well with water. In proteins, they tend to cluster together in the interior, away from the aqueous environment — like oil droplets avoiding water.

items.definition: The simplest amino acid — just a hydrogen atom as its R group. This makes it extremely flexible and often found in tight turns.
A methyl group (-CH₃) side chain. Small and commonly found in alpha helices.
Branched-chain amino acids with larger hydrocarbon groups. Important for muscle protein synthesis.
Contain aromatic rings. Tryptophan is the largest amino acid and a precursor to serotonin.
Unique cyclic structure where the side chain bonds back to the backbone nitrogen. Creates kinks in protein chains.
Contains sulfur. Often the first amino acid in newly synthesized proteins (start codon = AUG = Met).

children.children: Polar amino acids have side chains that can form hydrogen bonds with water. They're often found on protein surfaces, making proteins soluble.

items.definition: Contain hydroxyl groups (-OH). Common sites for phosphorylation — a key regulatory mechanism in cells.
Contains a thiol group (-SH). Two cysteines can form disulfide bonds (-S-S-), which stabilize protein structure.
Has a phenol ring with -OH. Important in signaling pathways and can be phosphorylated.
Contain amide groups. Glutamine is the most abundant amino acid in blood plasma.

props.title: Deep Dive: Charged Amino Acids (Acidic & Basic)

children.children: Charged amino acids carry electrical charge at physiological pH (7.4). They form ionic bonds and are crucial for enzyme active sites and protein-protein interactions.
Acidic (Negatively Charged)

items.definition: One-carbon spacer + carboxyl group. Often in enzyme active sites.
Two-carbon spacer + carboxyl group. Also a neurotransmitter (glutamic acid). MSG = monosodium glutamate!

children.children: Basic (Positively Charged)

items.definition: Long side chain ending in -NH₃⁺. Important for histone modifications that regulate gene expression.
Guanidinium group — strongly basic. Common in DNA-binding proteins.
Imidazole ring with pKa near 6. Can switch between charged/uncharged — perfect for enzyme catalysis.

children.children: Memory trick: Acidic amino acids (D, E) are 'negative nancys' and basic amino acids (K, R, H) are 'positive pals.' The charged residues love to interact with each other through salt bridges!
The Universal Backbone
Every amino acid shares the same core structure — a central alpha carbon (Cα) bonded to four different groups:

items.definition: The nitrogen terminus (N-terminus). At physiological pH, it's protonated: -NH₃⁺
The carbon terminus (C-terminus). At physiological pH, it's deprotonated: -COO⁻
The central carbon that connects everything. Called 'alpha' because it's adjacent to the carboxyl carbon.
The variable part that makes each amino acid unique. Ranges from a single hydrogen (glycine) to complex ring structures (tryptophan).
A single H attached to the alpha carbon (except in proline, where the side chain loops back).

children.children:
R (side chain)
        |
   H₃N⁺—Cα—COO⁻
        |
        H

At physiological pH (7.4), amino acids exist as
zwitterions: positively charged amino group,
negatively charged carboxyl group.

Chirality matters! All amino acids (except glycine) are chiral — they have 'left-handed' (L) and 'right-handed' (D) forms. Life almost exclusively uses L-amino acids. D-amino acids are found in some bacterial cell walls and antibiotics.

DNA uses 3-nucleotide codons to specify amino acids. With 4 bases (A, T, G, C), there are 4³ = 64 possible codons. This is more than enough for 20 amino acids, so the genetic code is degenerate — multiple codons can specify the same amino acid.

Some organisms have expanded this alphabet. Selenocysteine (the 21st amino acid) and pyrrolysine (the 22nd) are incorporated via special mechanisms. Scientists have even engineered organisms to incorporate completely synthetic amino acids!
Dehydration Synthesis: Building the Chain

When two amino acids join, they form a peptide bond through a dehydration synthesis (also called condensation) reaction. The carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing a water molecule.

Amino Acid 1      Amino Acid 2
    |                  |
  -COOH    +    H₂N-   →   -CO-NH-   +   H₂O
    ↑                ↑         ↑           ↑
Carboxyl        Amino      Peptide     Water
  group         group       bond      released

The C-N bond formed is called the peptide bond.
It has partial double-bond character (planar, rigid).

Think of it like connecting Lego bricks. Each amino acid has a 'male' end (amino group) and a 'female' end (carboxyl group). When you snap them together, a small piece (water) pops off to make room for the connection. The chain always grows in one direction: N-terminus → C-terminus.

In cells, peptide bonds are formed by ribosomes using ATP energy. The reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable, so it requires energy input. Breaking peptide bonds (hydrolysis) releases energy and is used by proteases to digest proteins.
The peptide bond has some remarkable properties due to resonance:

items.definition: Electrons are delocalized between C=O and C-N, giving the bond ~40% double-bond character. This makes rotation restricted.
The six atoms involved (Cα-C-O-N-H-Cα) lie in a plane. This constrains how the protein chain can fold.
Almost all peptide bonds are trans (the two Cα carbons on opposite sides). Cis bonds are rare (~0.03%) except before proline (~5%).
The C=O can accept H-bonds; the N-H can donate them. These interactions drive secondary structure formation.

children.children: Naming Peptides
Peptides are named by their length:
Each peptide bond releases one water molecule. So a protein with 100 amino acids released 99 water molecules during synthesis. This is why it's called 'dehydration' synthesis!
Proteins have four levels of structural organization, each building on the previous:
Primary Structure: The Sequence

The primary structure is simply the linear sequence of amino acids, read from N-terminus to C-terminus. It's determined directly by the gene sequence and written using single-letter or three-letter codes.

Single-letter: MVLSPADKTNVKAAWGK...
Three-letter:  Met-Val-Leu-Ser-Pro-Ala-Asp-Lys-Thr-Asn-Val-Lys-Ala-Ala-Trp-Gly-Lys...

This is the beginning of human hemoglobin alpha chain.
The sequence determines everything else about the protein.
Secondary Structure: Local Folding
Secondary structures are regular, repeating patterns stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms (not side chains).

items.definition: A right-handed coil where each C=O hydrogen-bonds to the N-H four residues ahead. Creates a rod-like structure with side chains pointing outward. Common in membrane proteins.
Extended strands lying side-by-side, connected by H-bonds between strands. Can be parallel (same direction) or antiparallel (opposite directions). Forms flat, rigid structures.
A tight turn that reverses the chain direction, often connecting β-strands. Usually 4 residues; proline and glycine are common.
Regions without regular secondary structure. Not actually random — still constrained, just not repetitive.

props.title: α-Helix vs β-Sheet: When Does Each Form?

children.children: The amino acid sequence determines which secondary structure forms:
Tertiary Structure: 3D Shape
Tertiary structure is the complete three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide chain. It results from interactions between side chains (R groups):

items.definition: Nonpolar side chains cluster in the protein interior, away from water. This is often the dominant force in protein folding.
Covalent bonds between two cysteine residues (-S-S-). Provide structural stability, especially in extracellular proteins.
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged side chains (e.g., Lys⁺ with Glu⁻).
Between polar side chains. Weaker than covalent bonds but numerous.
Weak attractions between all atoms at close range. Small individually but significant in aggregate.

children.children: Anfinsen's dogma: The amino acid sequence contains all the information needed to fold into the correct 3D structure. Christian Anfinsen won the 1972 Nobel Prize for demonstrating this with ribonuclease A.
Quaternary Structure: Multi-Chain Complexes
Quaternary structure describes how multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) assemble into a functional complex. Not all proteins have quaternary structure — only those with multiple subunits.

items.definition: 4 subunits (2α + 2β), each carrying one heme group. Cooperative binding of oxygen.
4 chains (2 heavy + 2 light) forming a Y-shape. Linked by disulfide bonds.
Multiple subunits forming a rotary motor. F₁ head + Fₒ membrane portion.
3 polypeptide chains wound into a triple helix. The most abundant protein in your body.

children.children: Think of the four structure levels like building a house: Primary = the list of building materials. Secondary = assembling materials into walls and beams (repeated patterns). Tertiary = arranging walls into a complete room (3D shape of one unit). Quaternary = combining rooms into a complete house (multiple units working together).

🧪 Interactive Demo: Use the Peptides app (/peptides) to build your own peptide chains! Select amino acids, watch dehydration synthesis release water molecules, and see how the chain grows from N-terminus to C-terminus.
The interactive peptide builder lets you:
Select from all 20 standard amino acids, color-coded by type
Watch peptide bonds form with water release animation
View the molecular structure with backbone and side chains
See the single-letter sequence grow as you add residues
Count the water molecules released during synthesis
Build these famous peptides in the interactive builder:

items.definition: The simplest tripeptide. Glycine's small size allows tight turns.
Alternating pattern found in silk fibroin (spider silk!).
Nuclear localization signal — tells proteins to enter the nucleus.
Pattern that can form a zinc finger when two cysteines bind zinc.

children.children: By the Numbers

rows: All encoded by the universal genetic code
All made from just 20 amino acids!
Muscle protein spanning half a sarcomere
Two chains (A: 21, B: 30) linked by disulfide bonds

children.children: Titin is a molecular spring that gives muscles their elasticity. At 34,350 amino acids and ~3.8 million Daltons, it's so large that its gene name is the longest word in the English language (189,819 letters if you spelled it out chemically).
Fun fact: Titin is so long that ribosomes take over 30 minutes to synthesize a single molecule. If stretched out, one titin molecule would be about 1 micrometer long — the size of a small bacterium!

The protein folding problem: How does a chain of amino acids find its correct 3D structure among astronomical possibilities? A 100-residue protein has roughly 10^100 possible conformations, yet folds in milliseconds. This paradox (Levinthal's paradox) shows that proteins don't search randomly — they follow energetically favorable folding pathways.
Protein Misfolding and Disease
When proteins misfold, the consequences can be severe:

items.definition: Amyloid-β peptides aggregate into plaques between neurons.
α-synuclein misfolds and forms Lewy bodies in brain cells.
Misfolded prion proteins (PrPSc) convert normal proteins to the diseased form. Includes mad cow disease, CJD.
Single amino acid change (Glu → Val) in hemoglobin causes aggregation under low oxygen.
Most common mutation (ΔF508) prevents CFTR protein from folding correctly.

children.children: Summary: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. Each has an amino group, carboxyl group, alpha carbon, and unique side chain. They link via peptide bonds (dehydration synthesis), forming chains that fold into functional 3D structures through four levels of organization.

items.definition: Like letters forming words, sequence determines function.
-COOH + H₂N- → -CO-NH- + H₂O. One water released per bond.
Primary (sequence) → Secondary (α-helix, β-sheet) → Tertiary (3D fold) → Quaternary (multi-chain).
Anfinsen's dogma: all folding information is in the amino acid sequence.
Misfolded proteins cause diseases from Alzheimer's to sickle cell anemia.
